skill.name: deployment-checker
skill.description: Validate release readiness with deployment, security, performance, infrastructure, and rollback checks.
skill.summary: Generate structured pre-deployment readiness checks and release recommendations.
skill.author: CuriousLearner

security_audit.summary: Static analysis found many high-risk command execution and environment access patterns, but review shows they are in documentation and a Node.js sample deployment checker. The sample uses hardcoded local validation commands and checks secret variable presence without printing values, so no malicious intent or exfiltration was found. Publication is acceptable with a warning that automated examples should only run in trusted repositories.

medium_findings.title: Sample Checker Executes Local Deployment Commands
medium_findings.description: The Node.js example runs npm test, npm run build, npm audit, git secrets --scan, and npm run db:check-migrations through execSync. The commands are hardcoded and appropriate for deployment validation, but running them in an untrusted repository can execute project-defined scripts.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The execSync wrapper and command calls are directly present in the sample code. Confidence is high for local command execution risk, but lower than critical because no user-controlled command string or malicious destination was found.

low_findings.title: Environment Variable Presence Checks Are Limited
low_findings.description: The sample checks DATABASE_URL, JWT_SECRET, API_KEY, REDIS_URL, and FORCE_HTTPS for deployment readiness. It does not print secret values or send them elsewhere, so this is a legitimate but sensitive operational check.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The environment access and secret variable names are explicit. The code only tests presence, so the residual risk is low and depends on where the sample is run.

low_findings.title: Markdown Backtick Detections Are False Positives
low_findings.description: Ruby or shell backtick detections occur in Markdown code fences, usage examples, and report template formatting. They are not Ruby execution syntax in an executable Ruby file.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines are Markdown fences or inline formatting in SKILL.md. There is no Ruby source file or executable backtick expression at these locations.

low_findings.title: Weak Crypto And Reconnaissance Detections Are False Positives
low_findings.description: The weak cryptography hits do not show cryptographic operations, and the reconnaissance hits refer to SSL certificate checklist content. No evidence found of hash cracking, weak crypto use, or host reconnaissance behavior.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Manual review of the cited lines found descriptive deployment checklist text and a string includes check. The finding is limited by the static analyzer patterns, not by dangerous behavior.

dangerous_patterns.title: Synchronous Child Process Execution In Example Code
dangerous_patterns.description: The sample imports child_process and calls execSync(command). This is acceptable for a local deployment checker when commands are trusted, but it should not be used with untrusted command strings.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The child_process import and execSync call are explicit. The risk is contextual because current callers pass fixed commands.

dangerous_patterns.title: Deployment Secret Variable Names In Example Code
dangerous_patterns.description: The sample references common sensitive configuration names to verify release readiness. It checks only whether values exist and does not expose the values in output.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The environment variable names and process.env access are directly visible. The absence of value logging or network transfer reduces confidence in any severe issue.

content.value_statement: Deployment releases can fail when teams miss security, database, infrastructure, or rollback checks. This skill creates a structured readiness review with a go or no-go recommendation.
actual_capabilities: Builds pre-deployment checklists for code quality, dependencies, database changes, and environment configuration.
Reviews security readiness items such as secrets handling, HTTPS, headers, authentication, authorization, and rate limiting.
Assesses performance and infrastructure readiness using health checks, monitoring, alerts, backups, and resource limits.
Creates deployment report sections with status, blockers, action items, rollback plans, and sign-off details.
Provides sample automation logic for local checks such as tests, builds, audits, secrets scans, and migration readiness.
limitations: It cannot prove production readiness without accurate project data and current deployment context.
The automated script is an example and may need changes for each repository or CI/CD platform.
It does not replace manual approval from engineering, security, QA, or product owners.
Commands in the sample should only run in trusted repositories and controlled environments.

use_cases.title: Prepare a Production Release
use_cases.description: Create a release checklist that covers tests, build status, database migrations, security checks, monitoring, and rollback readiness.

use_cases.title: Review Deployment Risk
use_cases.description: Convert release evidence into a go or no-go recommendation with blockers, mitigations, and post-deployment monitoring needs.

use_cases.title: Standardize Team Sign-Off
use_cases.description: Generate a repeatable readiness report for technical, QA, DevOps, and product approval before deployment.

prompt_templates.title: Create a Basic Checklist
prompt_templates.prompt: Use deployment-checker to create a pre-deployment checklist for a small web application release to staging.

prompt_templates.title: Assess Production Readiness
prompt_templates.prompt: Use deployment-checker to assess production readiness for this release. Include tests, build, dependencies, database migrations, environment variables, security, performance, monitoring, and rollback.

prompt_templates.title: Build a Go No-Go Report
prompt_templates.prompt: Use deployment-checker to produce a go or no-go report from our release notes, CI results, security scan summary, migration plan, and rollback plan.

prompt_templates.title: Design a Deployment Gate
prompt_templates.prompt: Use deployment-checker to design a deployment readiness gate for our CI/CD workflow. Separate automated checks, manual approvals, blockers, warnings, and post-deployment monitoring.

output_examples.input: Production API release with passing tests, one documentation warning, tested migrations, and a rollback plan.
output_examples.output: The result is an approved deployment report. It lists completed critical checks, two recommended follow-up items, tested rollback steps, and a low overall risk rating.

output_examples.input: Frontend release with failed accessibility tests, missing monitoring alerts, and no confirmed rollback owner.
output_examples.output: The result is a no-go recommendation. It marks failed accessibility checks and missing operational ownership as blockers before deployment.

output_examples.input: Database migration release with backup completed, staging test passed, but rollback scripts not verified.
output_examples.output: The result is a conditional release review. It requires rollback verification before approval and recommends close monitoring after migration.

best_practices: Run readiness checks in staging before production and record evidence for each critical item.
Verify rollback steps, monitoring alerts, and ownership before approving the release window.
Keep release checklists specific to the application, environment, data changes, and deployment method.
anti_patterns: Do not approve deployment when critical tests, migrations, or rollback plans are missing.
Do not run automated command examples in untrusted repositories or unknown project workspaces.
Do not treat a generic checklist as proof that production dependencies and secrets are configured correctly.

faq.question: Does this skill deploy my application?
faq.answer: No. It helps evaluate readiness and create reports, but it does not perform the deployment by itself.

faq.question: Can it work with any deployment platform?
faq.answer: Yes, the checklist is platform-neutral. You should adapt commands and evidence to your CI/CD system and infrastructure.

faq.question: Does it verify secrets safely?
faq.answer: It can check whether required variables are documented or present. It should not print, store, or share secret values.

faq.question: Can it produce a go or no-go decision?
faq.answer: Yes. It can organize blockers, warnings, completed checks, risk level, and final recommendation.

faq.question: Is the automated script ready to use unchanged?
faq.answer: Not always. Treat it as a starting point and adjust commands, thresholds, and required variables for your project.

faq.question: Who should use this skill?
faq.answer: Release managers, DevOps engineers, engineering leads, and teams that need repeatable deployment readiness reviews can use it.
